Presolve Reductions in MIP — Worked Examples

Preface

This is a worked-example tour of the presolve techniques used in modern mixed-integer programming solvers. For each reduction it provides

  1. A short statement of what the reduction does and the condition under which it fires.
  2. A tiny hand-crafted LP/MIP instance on which the reduction is the protagonist.
  3. The model after the reduction has been applied — so you can read off, by hand, what changed.

The techniques are organized following the taxonomy in

Tobias Achterberg, Robert E. Bixby, Zonghao Gu, Edward Rothberg, Dieter Weninger. Presolve Reductions in Mixed Integer Programming. ZIB Report 16-44 (September 2016). PDF · landing page

which remains the most thorough single reference on the topic. The chapter numbering matches the paper's section numbering one-to-one (chapter 3.5 here is paper §3.5), so the book can be read alongside the paper as a hands-on companion. The focus, however, is the technique itself: the math, the structure that makes it fire, and the shape of the resulting reduction. Solver-specific tuning and benchmark numbers are out of scope.

The instances are stored as standalone .lp files alongside each chapter, so they can be loaded into any MIP solver (SCIP, HiGHS, Gurobi, CPLEX, …) and inspected with the solver's presolve log. The book embeds them into the chapters via mdBook's {{#include}} directive — the chapter you read and the file you can solve are the same bytes on disk.

How to read this book

Each chapter is self-contained and follows the same structure: the reduction, a trigger condition, an instance, a walkthrough, and the post-presolve model. Subtle conditions and edge cases that any implementation must handle (numerical safeguards, integrality requirements, fill-in concerns) appear in the closing notes.

Status

The book is a work in progress. Two reference chapters — §3.5 and §4.5 — are fully written and serve as the format template. Every other chapter starts with a DRAFT — unreviewed banner: the prose was scaffolded automatically and the math, instance, and explanation have not yet been checked. We are reviewing these one at a time, replacing each draft with a worked-through chapter as we go.

Notation

We follow the paper's notation throughout. Restating the essentials:

The mixed integer program

Given a matrix A∈Rm×n, vectors c∈Rn and b∈Rm, lower bounds ℓ∈(R∪{−∞})n and upper bounds u∈(R∪{∞})n, an integer index set I⊆N={1,…,n}, and relations ∘i​∈{=,≤,≥} for each row i∈M={1,…,m}, the mixed integer program is

min s.t. ​cTxAx∘bℓ≤x≤uxj​∈Zfor all j∈I.​

We write Ai⋅​ for row i, A⋅j​ for column j, and supp(Ai⋅​)={j∈N:aij​=0} for the support of a row.

Row activities

The minimal and maximal activity of row i are

inf{Ai⋅​x}=j:aij​>0∑​aij​ℓj​+j:aij​<0∑​aij​uj​,sup{Ai⋅​x}=j:aij​>0∑​aij​uj​+j:aij​<0∑​aij​ℓj​,

with the conventions s⋅∞=∞ for s>0 etc. These activities are the workhorse quantities of single-row presolve.

Tolerances

The feasibility tolerance ε controls when an inequality is considered redundant or infeasible; in Gurobi, ε=10−6 by default. The constant ψ=1030 is the convention for "infinity" in the model data.

Conversion between ≤ and ≥ is by negation, so without loss of generality the descriptions in this book consider only one direction of each inequality.

Reductions for individual constraints

Single-row reductions look at one constraint at a time, using only the row's coefficients Ai⋅​, its right-hand side bi​, the variable bounds ℓ,u, and the integrality of the variables. Because they never combine information across rows, they are the cheapest reductions in the toolbox — and because they apply equally to the LP relaxation, every LP presolver implements them too.

SectionTechniqueWhat it does
3.1Model cleanup and removal of redundant constraintsDiscards rows whose activity bounds already imply them; detects infeasibility from a single row.
3.2Bound strengthening (single-row domain propagation)Tightens variable bounds using row activities.
3.3Coefficient strengtheningReplaces a row by a dominating row that is equivalent over integers but tighter in the LP.
3.4Chvátal–Gomory strengtheningScales an integer row by s>0 and rounds, when the rounded row dominates the original.
3.5Euclidean and modular inverse reductionDivides an integer row by the gcd of its coefficients; uses modular inverses to substitute structurally constrained integer variables.
3.6Simple probing on a single equationWhen an equation is "tight" against a binary variable, substitutes every other variable in the row in terms of that binary.
3.7Special ordered set reductionsFixings, deletions, and structural simplifications inside a single SOS1 or SOS2 constraint.

Individually each is modest; collectively they account for a substantial fraction of the speed-up presolve provides on real models.

3.1 Model cleanup and removal of redundant constraints

Paper §3.1

The reduction

Recall (see notation) the row activity bounds inf{Ai⋅​x}=j:aij​>0∑​aij​ℓj​+j:aij​<0∑​aij​uj​, sup{Ai⋅​x}=j:aij​>0∑​aij​uj​+j:aij​<0∑​aij​ℓj​. Every feasible point assigns row i an activity somewhere in [inf,sup].

For a ≤-inequality Ai⋅​x≤bi​, two outcomes are immediate from these bounds:

  • if sup{Ai⋅​x}≤bi​+ε, the row is redundant — every feasible point already satisfies it, so the row can be dropped without changing the feasible region;
  • if inf{Ai⋅​x}>bi​+ε, the row is infeasible — no feasible point exists, so the whole model is infeasible from this single row.

For an equation Ai⋅​x=bi​ both checks apply: redundant if inf≥bi​−ε and sup≤bi​+ε; infeasible if either strict inequality fails.

The check looks only at the row's own coefficients and the global variable bounds — no interaction with other constraints. Multi-row redundancy (a row implied by several other rows working together) is a separate, more expensive reduction; see §5.1.

Despite its simplicity, this reduction fires very often: the paper reports that on its test set, 2525 out of 3182 models contain at least one row that can be classified as redundant using only the original variable bounds.

Instance

\ §3.1 Model cleanup and removal of redundant constraints
\
\ Constraint c2 is redundant: sup{x + y} over the bounds is 10 + 10 = 20,
\ well below the right-hand side of 100. The constraint can be dropped
\ without changing the feasible region.

Minimize
 obj: x + 2 y
Subject To
 c1: x + y >= 3
 c2: x + y <= 100
Bounds
 0 <= x <= 10
 0 <= y <= 10
End

What fires

Compute the activity bounds of c2. The support of c2 is {x,y} with both coefficients positive (ax​=ay​=1), so by the formula above sup{c2​}=1⋅ux​+1⋅uy​=10+10=20. The right-hand side is 100, and 20≤100+ε. So c2 is redundant — every feasible point already satisfies it — and the row is dropped.

For comparison, c1 has inf{c1​}=0 and sup{c1​}=20 against right-hand side 3. Neither 0>3 (infeasibility) nor 20<3 (redundancy for ≥) holds, so the row stays.

Post-presolve model

\ After dropping the redundant constraint c2.
\ The remaining model is the same LP without that row.

Minimize
 obj: x + 2 y
Subject To
 c1: x + y >= 3
Bounds
 0 <= x <= 10
 0 <= y <= 10
End

The model shrinks from two constraints to one. The optimal solution is unchanged: x⋆=3, y⋆=0 with objective value 3.

Notes

  • Infeasibility detection is the same reduction, run the other way. If the right-hand side of c2 were −1 instead of 100, then inf{c2​}=0>−1+ε would prove the model infeasible from this single row, with no other reasoning required.
  • Coefficient cleanup. A secondary flavor of "cleanup" zeros coefficients whose magnitude is so small that their largest possible contribution to row activity is bounded by a fraction of the feasibility tolerance. The model is mathematically unchanged within tolerance — the row's activity bounds shift by at most ε — but the constraint matrix gets sparser and better scaled, which speeds up downstream LP solves and reduces round-off in the simplex factorization. The thresholds are numerical and depend on the implementation; the idea is just "if a coefficient cannot move the row activity by more than a noise-level amount, treat it as zero."
  • Why it matters. Redundant rows do nothing for the LP relaxation but they still cost time in every iteration of the simplex method, every cut-separation pass, and every node propagation step. Each individual row removal is a small constant-factor speed-up; the cumulative effect on real models is large.

3.2 Bound strengthening

Paper §3.2

The reduction

Take a single inequality Ai⋅​x≤bi​ and pick one variable xk​ in its support. Split the row into "xk​ alone" and "everything else": AiS​xS​+aik​xk​≤bi​,S=supp(Ai⋅​)∖{k}. The activity of the "everything else" block over its bounds is, at minimum, ℓiS​:=inf{AiS​xS​}=j∈S, aij​>0∑​aij​ℓj​+j∈S, aij​<0∑​aij​uj​. Whatever happens elsewhere in the row, AiS​xS​ is at least ℓiS​, so xk​ can contribute at most bi​−ℓiS​ to the row's activity. Dividing by aik​ gives a bound on xk​:

  • if aik​>0, then xk​≤(bi​−ℓiS​)/aik​, tightening the upper bound: uk​:=min{uk​, aik​bi​−ℓiS​​};
  • if aik​<0, then xk​≥(bi​−ℓiS​)/aik​ (the inequality flips because we divided by a negative), tightening the lower bound: ℓk​:=max{ℓk​, aik​bi​−ℓiS​​}.

For integer xk​ the new bound is rounded inward (⌊⋅⌋ for an upper bound, ⌈⋅⌉ for a lower bound), since fractional values aren't achievable anyway.

The check requires ℓiS​ to be finite; if any j∈S has ℓj​=−∞ on a positive coefficient (or uj​=+∞ on a negative one), then ℓiS​=−∞ and the formula gives no information.

This is the simplest form of domain propagation — the workhorse of every constraint propagation engine.

Instance

\ §3.2 Bound strengthening
\
\ The constraint 4x + 3y <= 15 implies an upper bound on x:
\   4x <= 15 - 3 * inf{y} = 15 - 0 = 15
\   x <= 15 / 4 = 3.75
\ Since x is integer, the bound rounds inward to u_x = 3.

Minimize
 obj: x + y
Subject To
 c1: 4 x + 3 y <= 15
Bounds
 0 <= x <= 10
 0 <= y <= 5
General
 x
End

What fires

Apply the formula to constraint c1 with k=x.

  • Set S={y}, so AiS​=(3).
  • Coefficient sign: ay​=3>0, contribution to the lower activity is 3⋅ℓy​=3⋅0=0. Therefore ℓiS​=0.
  • Coefficient on the target variable: ax​=4>0, so we tighten an upper bound: ux​≤415−0​=3.75.
  • x is integer, so round inward: ux​:=⌊3.75⌋=3.

The variable's upper bound moves from 10 to 3.

Repeating the formula for k=y (just to confirm there is nothing more to do): uy​≤315−0​=5, which matches the existing upper bound uy​=5. No change.

Post-presolve model

\ After single-row bound strengthening on c1 against x.
\ The constraint is unchanged; only the upper bound u_x tightens from 10 to 3.

Minimize
 obj: x + y
Subject To
 c1: 4 x + 3 y <= 15
Bounds
 0 <= x <= 3
 0 <= y <= 5
General
 x
End

The constraint itself is unchanged; only x's upper bound has been tightened.

Notes

  • Cascading. A single round of bound strengthening can trigger more in the next round: tightening ux​ makes some other row's ℓiS​ tighter (because ux​ entered with a negative coefficient, say), which then tightens some third variable's bound, and so on. Practical implementations iterate the procedure across all rows until no further changes are observed.
  • Infinite chains are possible on continuous models. The paper gives the example x1​−ax2​=0,ax1​−x2​=0,0≤x1​,x2​≤1,0<a<1. Starting from ux1​​=1, the first equation tightens ux1​​≤a, the second then gives ux2​​≤a2, the first then gives ux1​​≤a3, and so on — an infinite sequence of strictly smaller bounds converging to 0, but never finishing. Practical implementations guard against this by capping the number of passes, requiring a minimum step size on each update, and bounding the absolute magnitude of any new bound. Integer models do not have this issue because each tightening of an integer bound moves it by at least one.
  • Infeasibility as a side effect. If a derived upper bound is less than the current lower bound (or vice versa), the model is infeasible at this constraint.

3.3 Coefficient strengthening

Paper §3.3

The reduction

Take an inequality with an integer variable xk​: AiS​xS​+aik​xk​≤bi​,S=supp(Ai⋅​)∖{k}, xk​∈Z. Assume aik​>0 and uk​<∞. Let uiS​:=sup{AiS​xS​}=j∈S, aij​>0∑​aij​uj​+j∈S, aij​<0∑​aij​ℓj​ be the maximum activity of the rest of the row (the mirror image of the ℓiS​ used in §3.2).

Now compute d:=bi​−uiS​−aik​(uk​−1). This is the slack of the constraint at xk​=uk​−1 when the rest of the row is at its maximum activity. If d>0 and aik​≥d, the constraint can be replaced by the strengthened version AiS​xS​+(aik​−d)xk​≤bi​−duk​.(⋆)

Why this works

Compare the two constraints at each integer value of xk​:

xk​Original RHS for AiS​xS​Strengthened RHS for AiS​xS​
uk​bi​−aik​uk​(bi​−duk​)−(aik​−d)uk​=bi​−aik​uk​
uk​−1bi​−aik​(uk​−1)(bi​−duk​)−(aik​−d)(uk​−1)=bi​−aik​(uk​−1)−d=uiS​
≤uk​−2even loosereven looser

At xk​=uk​ the two constraints say exactly the same thing. At xk​=uk​−1 the strengthened one says "AiS​xS​≤uiS​" — which is automatically satisfied because uiS​ is the maximum possible activity of AiS​xS​. The integer-feasible region is therefore unchanged, but the LP relaxation is strictly tighter: any LP point with xk​ fractional in (uk​−1,uk​) is now cut off if it relied on the original constraint's slack.

The key insight: the original constraint was "loose at xk​=uk​−1 but not at xk​=uk​", and the strengthening levels the two cases by transferring slack from the coefficient on xk​ to the right-hand side.

The mirror case (aik​<0, tightening at the lower bound) follows the same recipe with signs swapped.

Instance

\ §3.3 Coefficient strengthening
\
\ The binary variable z has coefficient 5 in the constraint.
\ The "rest of the row" 2x + 3y has maximum activity 2 + 3 = 5 over the bounds.
\ At z = 0 the constraint reads 2x + 3y <= 8 but the LHS can be at most 5,
\ so the constraint has 3 units of slack at z = 0.
\ Strengthening shrinks z's coefficient from 5 to 2 and the RHS from 8 to 5.

Minimize
 obj: z + x + y
Subject To
 c1: 5 z + 2 x + 3 y <= 8
Bounds
 0 <= z <= 1
 0 <= x <= 1
 0 <= y <= 1
General
 z
End

What fires

Apply the rule with k=z.

  • aik​=5>0, uk​=1.
  • S={x,y}, AiS​=(2,3). Both coefficients are positive, so uiS​=2⋅ux​+3⋅uy​=2⋅1+3⋅1=5.
  • Slack at xk​=uk​−1=0: d=bi​−uiS​−aik​(uk​−1)=8−5−5⋅0=3.
  • d=3>0 ✓ and aik​=5≥d=3 ✓ — both conditions hold.

Apply (⋆):

  • new coefficient on z: 5−3=2,
  • new right-hand side: 8−3⋅1=5.

The strengthened constraint is 2z+2x+3y≤5.

Post-presolve model

\ After coefficient strengthening on c1 against the binary variable z.
\
\ New coefficient on z:  a'_z = 5 - 3 = 2
\ New right-hand side:   b' = 8 - 3 * 1 = 5
\
\ The integer-feasible region is unchanged. The LP relaxation is strictly
\ tighter — e.g., the point (z, x, y) = (0.5, 1, 1) was LP-feasible in the
\ original constraint (5*0.5 + 2 + 3 = 7.5 <= 8) and is now cut off
\ (2*0.5 + 2 + 3 = 6 > 5).

Minimize
 obj: z + x + y
Subject To
 c1: 2 z + 2 x + 3 y <= 5
Bounds
 0 <= z <= 1
 0 <= x <= 1
 0 <= y <= 1
General
 z
End

Sanity-check the integer cases:

  • z=0: original gives 2x+3y≤8, new gives 2x+3y≤5. Since sup{2x+3y}=5, both are satisfied by every feasible (x,y)∈[0,1]2 — the integer-feasible region is the same.
  • z=1: original gives 2x+3y≤3, new gives 2x+3y≤3 — identical.

The LP cut is real: (z,x,y)=(0.5,1,1) was LP-feasible originally (5⋅0.5+2+3=7.5≤8) and is no longer feasible (2⋅0.5+2+3=6>5).

Notes

  • Integrality is essential. The strengthening exploits the gap between xk​=uk​−1 and xk​=uk​. For a continuous xk​ no such gap exists, and the constraint Ai⋅​x≤bi​ is already as tight as it can be in the LP.
  • When aik​=d. The new coefficient on xk​ becomes 0, so xk​ disappears from the row entirely. The constraint shrinks in support. This is more aggressive than just changing a number — it can trigger downstream singleton-column or implied-free substitutions.
  • When aik​<d. The bare strengthening formula would produce a negative coefficient on xk​, which is valid but unhelpful. In this case the original constraint is so loose at xk​=uk​−1 that bound strengthening (§3.2) on xk​ from the activity at xk​=uk​ gives a tighter result, so coefficient strengthening yields to that.
  • LP solvers cannot do this on their own. The strengthened constraint is a valid inequality for the integer-feasible region but not for the LP relaxation — it cuts off LP-feasible points. An LP solver, with no knowledge of integrality, has no reason to introduce it. The reduction is therefore an MIP-presolve speciality, valuable precisely because the LP relaxation gets stronger for free.

3.4 Chvátal–Gomory strengthening

Paper §3.4

The reduction

Take a ≥-inequality whose support consists entirely of non-negative integer variables: Ai⋅​x≥bi​,xj​∈Z≥0​ for all j∈supp(Ai⋅​). (If some coefficients are negative, complement the corresponding variables. If some lower bounds are non-zero, shift the variables so they are. The "all coefficients non-negative, all lower bounds zero" form is canonical for this reduction.)

For any positive scalar s>0, the Chvátal–Gomory cut is ⌈sAi⋅​⌉x≥⌈sbi​⌉.(CGs​) This is always valid for the integer-feasible region. Sometimes it is strictly tighter than the original constraint in the LP relaxation — in which case we replace the original with the CG cut.

Why the CG cut is valid

Two inequalities chained together:

  1. Multiply both sides of the original by s>0 (preserves the inequality direction): sAi⋅​x≥sbi​.
  2. Each coefficient satisfies saij​≤⌈saij​⌉, and multiplying by xj​≥0 preserves the inequality direction. Summing over j, sAi⋅​x≤⌈sAi⋅​⌉x. Combining: ⌈sAi⋅​⌉x≥sbi​.

Finally, the left-hand side is a sum of products of integer coefficients and integer variables, so it is itself integer. Any integer at least sbi​ is at least ⌈sbi​⌉. So ⌈sAi⋅​⌉x≥⌈sbi​⌉.

The two integrality assumptions — integer variables and non-negative bounds — are both essential. Drop either one and at least one step of the chain fails.

When the CG cut strictly tightens the LP

The CG cut and the original constraint compare on equal footing once we normalize both to the same RHS. Dividing (CGs​) through by ⌈sbi​⌉/bi​ gives the same RHS as the original; its coefficients become ⌈sbi​⌉⌈saij​⌉⋅bi​​. The CG cut dominates the original in the LP if and only if ⌈sbi​⌉⌈saij​⌉⋅bi​​≤aij​for every j,with strict inequality for some k. (Smaller coefficients on a ≥-inequality with x≥0 mean a tighter constraint.)

Choosing s

Picking s is heuristic: there is no closed-form choice. A typical implementation tries a small fixed set of candidates — for instance s∈{1,t/amax​,t/amin​,(2t−1)/(2amin​):t=1,…,5} where amax​,amin​ are the largest and smallest non-zero coefficients. If none of them produce a strict tightening, the row is left alone.

Instance

\ §3.4 Chvátal–Gomory strengthening
\
\ Three binary variables with distinct coefficients 6, 5, 4 on the left and
\ RHS 7 on the right. Each variable alone (at value 1) is too small to
\ satisfy the constraint, so any feasible point must have at least two of
\ the three variables set to 1.
\
\ The Chvátal–Gomory cut with scalar s = 1/6 captures this directly:
\   x + y + z >= 2

Minimize
 obj: x + y + z
Subject To
 c1: 6 x + 5 y + 4 z >= 7
Bounds
 0 <= x <= 1
 0 <= y <= 1
 0 <= z <= 1
General
 x y z
End

The combinatorial content: each variable alone, at value 1, contributes at most 6 to the row — strictly less than bi​=7. So at least two of x,y,z must be 1 in any feasible point. This "covering" structure is what the CG cut is going to capture.

What fires

Try s=1/6 (this corresponds to t/amax​ with t=1 in the heuristic set above).

  • s⋅aij​ for j=x,y,z is 1, 5/6, 4/6, all of which round up to 1. So ⌈sAi⋅​⌉=(1,1,1).
  • s⋅bi​=7/6, which rounds up to 2. So ⌈sbi​⌉=2.

The candidate CG cut is x+y+z≥2.

Dominance check — normalize the cut to RHS 7 by multiplying through by 7/2: 27​x+27​y+27​z≥7. Compare coefficient-by-coefficient against the original (6,5,4):

jOriginal aij​Normalized CG coefficientStrict?
x63.5✓
y53.5✓
z43.5✓

All three are strictly less. The dominance condition holds, so the original row is replaced by the CG cut.

Post-presolve model

\ After Chvátal–Gomory strengthening of c1 with scalar s = 1/6.
\
\ Original:  6 x + 5 y + 4 z >= 7
\ CG cut:    x + y + z >= 2
\
\ The CG cut dominates the original in the LP — every coefficient, after
\ normalizing the cut to RHS = 7, is strictly smaller than the original
\ coefficient (3.5 vs 6, 5, 4). The original constraint is replaced.

Minimize
 obj: x + y + z
Subject To
 c1: x + y + z >= 2
Bounds
 0 <= x <= 1
 0 <= y <= 1
 0 <= z <= 1
General
 x y z
End

The LP relaxation is genuinely tighter. For example, (x,y,z)=(1,0,0.5) was LP-feasible originally (6+0+2=8≥7) and is now cut off (1.5<2). And (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1) — the single-variable activations that satisfied the original constraint's LP relaxation but never any integer feasible solution — are all cut off as well.

Notes

  • Integrality is essential. Both pieces — integer variables and non-negative lower bounds — are needed to round both the LHS up to its ceiling and the RHS up to its ceiling. For a continuous variable, saij​xj​≤⌈saij​⌉xj​ still holds, but the LHS of the resulting inequality is no longer integer, so the RHS cannot be ceilinged.
  • Special case: s integer. If s is itself an integer, then ⌈saij​⌉=saij​ and ⌈sbi​⌉=sbi​, so the CG cut is just the original multiplied by s — no improvement. Useful values of s are fractional.
  • Relation to general Chvátal–Gomory cuts. The full CG procedure combines a non-negative linear combination of multiple constraints before rounding, which is how CG cuts are generated in branch-and-cut. The presolve version is the simplest case: one row, one multiplier. The combinatorial reach of the cut is correspondingly more modest, but it is also far cheaper to find.

3.5 Euclidean and modular inverse reduction

Paper §3.5

This section is really two reductions stacked on top of each other. The first — the Euclidean reduction — is simple. The second — the modular inverse substitution — needs a short detour through modular arithmetic before it lands cleanly.

The Euclidean reduction

Given a constraint with all-integer variables, j=1∑n​aij​xj​∘i​bi​,xj​∈Z, let d=gcd(ai1​,…,ain​) in the extended sense (the largest rational such that every aij​/d is an integer). Both sides of the constraint are multiples of d, so we can divide through: j=1∑n​daij​​xj​∘i​⌊dbi​​⌋. For an equation, infeasibility is detected immediately if bi​/d∈/Z: the left-hand side is an integer multiple of d but the right-hand side is not.

Example: 4x1​+6x2​=10 has d=gcd(4,6)=2, and we divide through to 2x1​+3x2​=5.

This is the Euclidean reduction — straightforward, fires whenever the row's coefficients share a common factor.

When the Euclidean reduction does nothing

What if the coefficients are already coprime — gcd=1? Then we cannot divide through and the Euclidean reduction is a no-op. But integer structure can still be hidden in the equation. Consider 1867x1​+1913x2​=3,618,894,x1​,x2​∈Z≥0​. The coefficients 1867 and 1913 are coprime (both are prime), so the Euclidean reduction has nothing to say. And yet we are about to discover that x1​ is forced to be of a very specific form.

The trick: reduce both sides of the equation modulo 1913, the coefficient of x2​. The 1913x2​ term vanishes (it's a multiple of 1913), leaving 1867x1​≡3,618,894(mod1913). Since 3,618,894=1891⋅1913+1411, the right-hand side reduces to 1411(mod1913), giving 1867x1​≡1411(mod1913). This is a one-variable congruence. If we could "divide both sides by 1867" in modular arithmetic, we would learn exactly what residue x1​ must take modulo 1913.

That "division" is the modular inverse.

Modular inverse, defined

In ordinary arithmetic, the inverse of a number a is the number a−1 such that a⋅a−1=1. In modular arithmetic the analogous definition is:

The modular multiplicative inverse of a modulo m is the integer a−1∣m∈{1,2,…,m−1} satisfying a⋅a−1∣m≡1(modm).

Read "a−1∣m" as "the inverse of a, mod m." It exists if and only if gcd(a,m)=1.

To check what the inverse means: in arithmetic mod 7, 3⋅5=15=2⋅7+1≡1(mod7), so 3−1∣7=5. Then to solve 3x≡4(mod7) we just multiply both sides by 5: x≡20≡6(mod7). The inverse "undoes" multiplication by a, exactly like ordinary division.

For our instance, 1867−1∣1913=1206 (computed once and for all using the extended Euclidean algorithm). Verifying: 1867⋅1206=2,251,602=1177⋅1913+1≡1(mod1913).✓

The modular inverse reduction

Equipped with the modular inverse, return to the congruence 1867x1​≡1411(mod1913) and multiply both sides by 1867−1∣1913=1206: x1​≡1206⋅1411(mod1913). Computing: 1206⋅1411=1,701,666=889⋅1913+1009, so x1​≡1009(mod1913).

So every feasible x1​ has the form x1​=1913z+1009for some z∈Z≥0​. This is an affine substitution. We replace x1​ throughout the model by 1913z+1009, where z is a fresh integer variable.

The same trick is applicable whenever an equation contains an integer variable x1​ whose coefficient ai1​ is coprime to dˉ:=gcd(ai2​,…,ain​), with dˉ≥2. The substitution is x1​:=dˉ⋅z+bˉ,z∈Z,bˉ≡ai1−1∣dˉ​⋅bi​(moddˉ). After substituting back, the equation's new coefficients all share the factor dˉ, so the (ordinary, non-modular) Euclidean reduction fires on the next pass.

Instance

\ Paper Example 2 — modular inverse reduction
\ Achterberg et al. 2016, §3.5
\
\ A two-variable integer equation where the coefficients are coprime
\ but the RHS is not divisible by either of them. The Euclidean reduction
\ alone cannot simplify the constraint; the modular inverse trick can.

Minimize
 obj: x1 + x2
Subject To
 c1: 1867 x1 + 1913 x2 = 3618894
Bounds
 x1 >= 0
 x2 >= 0
General
 x1 x2
End

What fires

We just did the math. Substitute x1​:=1913z+1009 back into the original equation: 1867⋅(1913z+1009)+1913x2​=3,618,894⟹3,571,571z+1913x2​=1,735,091. Now the coefficients 3,571,571=1867⋅1913 and 1913 share gcd=1913 — the Euclidean reduction now fires on the transformed row, dividing through to 1867z+x2​=907.

Post-presolve model

\ After the modular-inverse substitution x1 := 1913 z + 1009.
\
\ Substituting into the original equation gives 3571571 z + 1913 x2 = 1735091,
\ which divides through by gcd 1913 to yield 1867 z + x2 = 907.
\
\ Objective: x1 + x2 = (1913 z + 1009) + x2 = 1913 z + x2 + 1009.
\ The trailing +1009 is the objective constant (uncrush offset for x1).

Minimize
 obj: 1913 z + x2 + 1009
Subject To
 c1: 1867 z + x2 = 907
Bounds
 z >= 0
 x2 >= 0
General
 z x2
End

The reduced model has the unique optimal solution z=0, x2​=907 — since 1867z≤907 forces z=0. Uncrushing: x1​=1913⋅0+1009=1009, with optimal objective value 1009+907=1916.

Without this reduction, branch-and-bound on the original equation has to enumerate many candidate values of x1​ before stumbling on 1009 — the original instance has no useful LP structure to guide the search. The modular inverse compresses that combinatorial work into a single substitution at the root.

Notes

  • Where modular inverses come from. The extended Euclidean algorithm computes gcd(a,m) and, as a by-product, two integers s,t with sa+tm=gcd(a,m). When gcd=1, that says sa≡1(modm), so a−1∣m=smodm. This is exactly the same algorithm the Euclidean reduction itself uses, just instrumented to track the coefficients.
  • When the modular trick is even possible. The substitution requires that ai1​ have an absolute value distinct from every other coefficient in the row. If two coefficients share an absolute value, then dˉ=gcd(ai2​,…,ain​)=1 trivially, and no reduction follows.
  • One continuous variable is allowed. The reduction works even if exactly one variable in the equation is continuous, provided that variable plays the role of x1​ — its value is then locked to a deterministic affine function of an integer auxiliary, which downstream presolves can propagate.
  • Fractional coefficients. When the row's coefficients are rational rather than integral, the gcd has to be defined in the extended rational sense above. In practice this is approximated heuristically, since computing exact rational gcds in finite-precision arithmetic is brittle.

Reductions for individual variables

Single-column reductions look at one variable (column) at a time, using its column A⋅j​, objective coefficient cj​, bounds ℓj​,uj​, integrality, and the row infima/suprema inf{Ai⋅​x},sup{Ai⋅​x} for the rows in which it appears.

SectionTechniqueWhat it does
4.1Removal of fixed variablesEliminates variables with ℓj​=uj​, folding their contribution into RHS and objective.
4.2Rounding bounds of integer variablesTightens fractional bounds on integer variables to the next integer inside.
4.3Strengthen semi-continuous and semi-integer boundsRemoves the "semi" property when the bounds prove it redundant.
4.4Dual fixing, substitution, and bound strengtheningUses sign patterns of cj​ and A⋅j​ to fix variables to bounds, substitute against a binary, or derive new bounds via dual reasoning.
4.5Substitute implied free variablesWhen a variable's explicit bounds are redundant given the rest of the constraints, eliminates it via an equation containing it ("aggregator").

Of these, §4.5 is the most powerful — it eliminates entire variables and equations from the model in one shot, so it is often the dominant single-column reduction.

4.5 Substitute implied free variables

Paper §4.5

The reduction

Let ℓj​,uj​ be the explicit bounds of variable xj​, and let ℓˉj​,uˉj​ be the tightest bounds on xj​ that can be deduced from the other constraints by single-row bound strengthening (§3.2), without using the explicit bounds of xj​ itself. If [ℓˉj​, uˉj​]⊆[ℓj​, uj​], then xj​ is called an implied free variable: its explicit bounds carry no information beyond what the other constraints already enforce. The case ℓj​=−∞, uj​=∞ (a genuinely free variable) is the trivial special case.

If, in addition, there is an equation of the form AiS​xS​+aij​xj​=bi​,S⊆N∖{j}, aij​=0, then xj​ can be substituted out of the entire model: xj​:=aij​bi​−AiS​xS​​. The equation is consumed; xj​ is eliminated from every other row and from the objective. For integer xj​ the substitution requires S⊆I and aik​/aij​∈Z for all k∈S, otherwise the substituted expression is not guaranteed to be integer-valued.

This reduction is sometimes called the aggregator.

Instance

\ §4.5 Substitute implied free variables
\ Achterberg et al. 2016, §4.5
\
\ x1 has explicit bounds [0, 5].
\ Inequality (c1) and the upper bound x2 <= 5 imply x1 <= 5.
\ Inequality (c2) and the lower bound x3 >= 0 imply x1 >= 0.
\ So x1's implied bounds equal its explicit bounds — it is implied free.
\
\ Equation (c3) contains x1, so the aggregator can substitute it out.

Minimize
 obj: 2 x1 + x2 + x3
Subject To
 c1: x1 - x2 <= 0
 c2: x1 - x3 >= 0
 c3: x1 + x2 + x3 = 6
Bounds
 0 <= x1 <= 5
 0 <= x2 <= 5
 0 <= x3 <= 5
End

By bound propagation on the inequality x1​−x2​≤0 together with the explicit upper bound ux2​​=5: x1​≤x2​≤5, and similarly from x1​−x3​≥0 with ℓx3​​=0: x1​≥x3​≥0. So ℓˉx1​​=0 and uˉx1​​=5 — exactly matching the explicit bounds [0,5]. Therefore x1​ is implied free, and the equation x1​+x2​+x3​=6 provides the substitution.

What fires

Substitute x1​:=6−x2​−x3​ into every other row and the objective.

RowBeforeAfter
c1​x1​−x2​≤0(6−x2​−x3​)−x2​≤0 ⟹ 2x2​+x3​≥6
c2​x1​−x3​≥0(6−x2​−x3​)−x3​≥0 ⟹ x2​+2x3​≤6
c3​x1​+x2​+x3​=6(consumed by the substitution; removed)
obj2x1​+x2​+x3​2(6−x2​−x3​)+x2​+x3​=−x2​−x3​+12

The model shrinks from 3 variables / 3 constraints to 2 variables / 2 constraints, with a +12 objective constant carried forward.

Post-presolve model

\ After substituting x1 := 6 - x2 - x3 (using equation c3).
\
\ Original c1 (x1 - x2 <= 0)  becomes  6 - 2 x2 - x3 <= 0,  i.e. 2 x2 + x3 >= 6
\ Original c2 (x1 - x3 >= 0)  becomes  6 - x2 - 2 x3 >= 0,  i.e. x2 + 2 x3 <= 6
\ Original c3 is consumed by the substitution and removed.
\
\ Objective: 2 x1 + x2 + x3 = 2(6 - x2 - x3) + x2 + x3 = 12 - x2 - x3.
\ The +12 is the objective constant (uncrush offset).

Minimize
 obj: - x2 - x3 + 12
Subject To
 c1: 2 x2 + x3 >= 6
 c2: x2 + 2 x3 <= 6
Bounds
 0 <= x2 <= 5
 0 <= x3 <= 5
End

In a post-processing ("uncrush") step, the optimal x1​ is recovered from x2⋆​,x3⋆​ via x1⋆​=6−x2⋆​−x3⋆​.

Notes

  • Fill-in. Substituting xj​ out of the equation rewrites every row in which xj​ appeared as a linear combination of AiS​ and the original row coefficients. If xj​ has support in many rows, this can substantially increase the number of non-zero entries in the matrix. Practical implementations therefore guard the substitution with a fill-in budget and a numerical safeguard: if ∣aij​∣ is small relative to the largest coefficient in its row or column, the substitution is rejected to avoid magnifying round-off.
  • Numerical stability. Because the substitution divides by aij​, small pivots are dangerous in finite precision. The aggregator is a recurring source of numerical issues in presolve, and is often the first reduction to disable when a model fails to solve cleanly.
  • Integer variables. The divisibility requirement on the row's coefficients is restrictive enough that the reduction is rarely available for integer xj​ in practice; it is overwhelmingly applied to continuous variables.
  • Why "implied free" matters. Without the implied-free check, substituting xj​ would silently throw away the explicit bounds [ℓj​,uj​] — a correct optimization on the reduced model could then return a solution with xj​ outside its declared range. The check guarantees the bounds were already enforced elsewhere.

Reductions on multiple constraints

Multi-row reductions consider two or more rows simultaneously. They are typically more expensive than single-row reductions — pairwise comparisons scale quadratically in the number of rows in the worst case — and so practical implementations guard them with hash-based bucketing, work limits, and structural shortcuts.

SectionTechniqueWhat it does
5.1Redundancy detectionRemoves rows whose feasible region is implied by other rows: dependent equations (LU-rank), inequalities subsumed by stronger ones, set-packing constraints subsumed by larger cliques.
5.2Parallel and nearly parallel rowsDetects rows that are scalar multiples of one another (or differ only on singleton variables) and merges, deletes, or sharpens them.
5.3Non-zero cancellationAdds an equation to another row to reduce the number of non-zero coefficients in the matrix.
5.4Multi-row bound and coefficient strengtheningComputes activity bounds across multiple rows simultaneously (up to optimization-based bound tightening), enabling sharper §3.2 / §3.3 reductions.
5.5Clique mergingCombines several set-packing constraints whose underlying conflict-graph cliques can be unified into a single larger clique.

These reductions reshape the structure of the constraint matrix and frequently expose new single-row and single-column opportunities, so they are typically iterated with the cheaper reductions to fixed point.

Reductions on multiple variables

Multi-column reductions look at two or more columns simultaneously: their objective coefficients, bounds, integrality, and the rows in which they appear.

SectionTechniqueWhat it does
6.1Multi-column dual fixing for single equationsGeneralizes single-column dual fixing (§4.4) to a group of variables that jointly bracket a single equation.
6.2Fix redundant penalty variablesWhen several singleton variables play the role of "penalties" on one constraint, fixes the more expensive ones to their bounds when the cheaper ones already suffice.
6.3Parallel columnsDetects columns A⋅j​=λA⋅k​ and merges xj​,xk​ into a single aggregated variable.
6.4Dominated columnsWhen one column dominates another (cheaper objective, weaker effect on every row, integrality compatible), fixes the dominated variable to a bound.

Multi-column reductions are typically less powerful than multi-row reductions — there are usually fewer "dual" structural symmetries between variables than between constraints in a real model — but they are cheap to detect via hashing and worth running.

Full-problem reductions

Full-problem reductions consider the model as a whole. They include the most powerful — and most expensive — algorithms in the presolver, and typically run in the outermost loop only once or twice.

SectionTechniqueWhat it does
7.1Aggregate pairs of symmetric variablesDetects variable permutations that leave the model unchanged and aggregates each orbit into a single representative.
7.2ProbingTentatively fixes a binary variable to 0 and to 1, propagates each, and harvests the implied bound changes, fixings, and substitutions (with optional lifting of cuts).
7.3Disconnected componentsSplits the constraint matrix into independent sub-blocks and solves each separately.
7.4Biconnected componentsWhen a single "articulation" variable couples two otherwise-independent sub-blocks, probes the articulation and decomposes.
7.5Exploiting complementary slacknessUses LP duality to derive variable fixings or implied equalities that hold for every optimal solution.
7.6Implied integer detectionIdentifies continuous variables whose value is forced to be integer by the surrounding constraints, and marks them as integer for downstream cut generation and branching.

These reductions are global in scope and frequently change the model in ways that re-trigger the cheaper reductions, so the presolve loop iterates the whole stack until no further changes are made.

Node presolve

Node presolve is the lightweight presolve pass that runs at every node of the branch-and-bound tree, after a branching decision has been made and before the LP relaxation of the subproblem is solved. Unlike root presolve, node presolve does not modify the coefficient matrix or right-hand sides — it only tightens the local bounds of variables in the current subproblem.

SectionTechniqueWhat it does
8.1Bound strengthening at nodesRe-runs §3.2 domain propagation on the rows that contain the branching variable, and chains further reductions.
8.2Orbital fixingUses the symmetry group of the original model, restricted to the branching subspace, to fix variables in each orbit to a representative.
8.3Conflict propagationTreats conflict constraints learned from infeasible siblings as additional rows and propagates them locally.
8.4Global probing at local nodesProbes binary variables that became fractional in the node LP and harvests globally valid implied bounds.

Node presolve runs millions of times during a search, so each technique must be cheap per call. The benefit is cumulative: even a small per-node reduction in the variable domains substantially shrinks the subtree below.

Note on demonstrating these reductions. Node presolve fires during branch-and-bound, not at the root. A static .lp file alone cannot show the reduction in action; the chapters in this part describe the condition under which a reduction would fire, but a complete demonstration requires running a solver and inspecting the per-node log.

Unreviewed drafts

The chapters listed below have not yet been reviewed. Each one was scaffolded automatically — the math, instance, and walkthrough are unchecked.

In the main book table of contents (sidebar), unreviewed chapters appear in their normal section position as greyed-out placeholders that cannot be clicked. The links in this "Unreviewed drafts" part of the sidebar give you a clickable entry point so you can read each draft and give feedback.

Workflow

We review one chapter at a time:

  1. Pick a draft from the list (or the section that's next in the book).
  2. Read the existing draft prose and discuss what needs fixing.
  3. Build an instance.lp (and expected.lp) for the technique.
  4. Replace the draft with the final chapter.
  5. Update SUMMARY.md: promote the in-section placeholder to a real link, and remove the entry from the "Unreviewed drafts" list.

Each draft chapter starts with a > **DRAFT — unreviewed.** banner under its title — that banner disappears when the chapter is promoted.

3.6 Simple probing on a single equation

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §3.6

Consider an equation Ai⋅​x=bi​ that satisfies inf{Ai⋅​x}+sup{Ai⋅​x}=2bi​ (after complementing variables to make all coefficients non-negative). This says the right-hand side sits exactly midway between the row's minimum and maximum activity. Now suppose xk​ is a binary variable in the support with the largest coefficient, satisfying ∣aik​∣=sup{Ai⋅​x}−bi​. Then the equation forces

  • xk​=0⟹xj​=uj​ for every other j in the support, and
  • xk​=1⟹xj​=ℓj​ for every other j in the support.

Combining the two implications produces the explicit substitution xj​:=uj​−(uj​−ℓj​)xk​for all j∈supp(Ai⋅​)∖{k}. The equation is consumed and every other variable in it disappears, replaced by an affine function of the single binary xk​.

Paper Example 3 — 10x1​+x2​+…+x11​=10 with x1​∈{0,1} and xj​∈[0,1] for j=2,…,11 — is a clean instance: x1​=0 forces all other xj​=1, and x1​=1 forces all other xj​=0, giving the substitution xj​:=1−x1​.

3.7 Special ordered set reductions

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §3.7

A SOS1 constraint requires at most one variable in {x1​,…,xp​} to be non-zero. A SOS2 constraint requires at most two, and any two non-zero variables must be adjacent in the specified order. Looking at a single SOS constraint in isolation, several simplifications follow directly from the variables' bounds and integrality:

  • Fixing. A SOS1 with a member xk​ for which ℓk​>0 or uk​<0 forces xk​ to be the (only) non-zero one, so every other member must be fixed to zero.
  • Removing zero-fixed members. Variables already fixed to zero can be deleted from a SOS1. For SOS2 the deletion is restricted to members at the start or end of the sequence — deleting an interior zero-fixed member would break the adjacency relation that defines the constraint.
  • Trivializing. A SOS1 reduced to one remaining variable, or a SOS2 reduced to at most two remaining variables, no longer says anything and can be discarded.
  • SOS2 → SOS1. A SOS2 over exactly three variables (x1​,x2​,x3​) reduces to a SOS1 over (x1​,x3​), since adjacency forces the middle position to be the one non-zero singleton if any.
  • Discarding length-2 SOS1. A SOS1 of length 2 can be discarded entirely if the objective and constraint signs allow both members to be jointly decreased to zero in post-processing without making the model worse.

These are the only reductions available from a single SOS constraint; richer reasoning across SOS constraints belongs to the multi-row part.

4.1 Removal of fixed variables

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §4.1

A variable xj​ with ℓj​=uj​ is fixed: every feasible point assigns it the same value. Such a variable can be removed from the model by

  • subtracting A⋅j​ℓj​ from the right-hand side b, and
  • folding cj​ℓj​ into the objective constant.

After the substitution xj​ no longer appears in any constraint or in the objective, so its column is deleted. In post-processing, recovering xj​ is trivial — it is fixed.

Fixed variables arise both directly in the input (e.g., a binary variable forced by data to 0 or 1) and as a consequence of other reductions, especially bound strengthening (§3.2) collapsing [ℓj​,uj​] to a singleton.

4.2 Rounding bounds of integer variables

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §4.2

If xj​ is integer and ℓj​ or uj​ is fractional, the bound can be replaced by the closest integer inside the interval: ℓj​←⌈ℓj​⌉,uj​←⌊uj​⌋. The new bound preserves the integer-feasible region exactly. As a side effect, the row infima and suprema (§notation) become tighter, which can in turn enable further bound strengthening on other variables.

This reduction is essentially a definitional clean-up: most downstream MIP machinery assumes bounds on integer variables are themselves integral, and the reduction makes that assumption true.

4.3 Strengthen semi-continuous and semi-integer bounds

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §4.3

A semi-continuous variable takes either the value 0 or any value in a specified interval [ℓj′​,uj​] with ℓj′​≥0. A semi-integer variable adds an integrality requirement on top.

Two reductions follow from external evidence about the variable's range:

  • If presolve proves xj​≥ℓj′​>0 from the surrounding constraints, the value 0 is impossible, and xj​ becomes a regular continuous (or integer) variable on [max{ℓj​,ℓj′​},uj​].
  • If presolve proves xj​<ℓj′​, the only option in the disjunction is xj​=0, so the variable is fixed.

Additionally, a semi-continuous variable with ℓj′​=0 is just a regular variable: the "semi" property says nothing.

4.4 Dual fixing, substitution, and bound strengthening

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §4.4

Suppose all constraints are ≤ inequalities or equations and xj​ does not appear in any equation, i.e. aij​=0 for every i with ∘i​==. Dual fixing says:

  • If cj​≥0 and aij​≥0 for every constraint, then making xj​ smaller never hurts: in any optimal solution xj​ can be set to ℓj​. (If ℓj​=−∞ and cj​>0 the model is unbounded.)
  • The mirror case with cj​≤0 and aij​≤0 everywhere fixes xj​ to uj​.

The reasoning is purely about signs: pushing xj​ in the favorable direction of the objective can only help feasibility on every row in which it appears, so an optimal solution at the bound always exists.

A useful refinement applies when there is exactly one "wrong-sign" row r with arj​<0 (still in the cj​≥0 case) and a binary xk​ in the same row whose value already determines whether row r is tight. Then xj​ can be substituted as an affine function of xk​ rather than fixed.

In the general case where dual fixing does not directly fix xj​, the same sign argument produces a tighter bound: any value u~j​<uj​ for which the wrong-sign rows would still be satisfiable is a valid replacement upper bound.

Paper Examples 4 and 5 are clean instances for this chapter.

5.1 Redundancy detection

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §5.1

A constraint r is redundant if removing it does not change the feasible set of the model. Deciding redundancy in full generality is as hard as the model itself (NP-complete for MIP), but several restricted variants are polynomial:

  • Single-row redundancy — a row whose activity bounds already imply it. Already covered in §3.1.
  • LP-redundancy from one auxiliary row — for a candidate row r, checking if sup{Ar⋅​x:AS⋅​x∘S​bS​}≤br​ on a subset S that excludes r. This is an LP, polynomial in size, and a useful sweet spot.
  • Dependent equations — a set of equations {Ai⋅​x=bi​} may contain a row that is a linear combination of the others. A rank-revealing LU factorization detects such rows; a redundant equation 0=0 is removed, and an inconsistent reduction 0=b with b=0 proves infeasibility.
  • Inequality subsumption — an inequality AS​x≤b′ is subsumed by AS​x+dy≤b′′ if the activity of dy is provably ≥b′′−b′. The subsumed row can be discarded as it never cuts more than the subsumer.
  • Set-packing / clique subsumption — a set-packing constraint is dominated if every variable in it appears in a larger clique inequality elsewhere in the model.

5.2 Parallel and nearly parallel rows

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §5.2

Two rows q and r are parallel if there is a scalar s=0 with Aq⋅​=sAr⋅​. Several merges follow from the relationship between bq​ and sbr​:

  • Two equations. r is discarded if bq​=sbr​; otherwise the two equations are inconsistent and the model is infeasible.
  • One equation, one inequality. The inequality is implied if its right-hand side is on the loose side of the equation (sign-dependent on s); on the tight side, the model is infeasible.
  • Two inequalities. If s>0, the looser of the two is implied by the tighter and is discarded. If s<0, the two inequalities together collapse to a ranged row sbr​≤Aq⋅​x≤bq​ (or, if the bounds coincide, an equation), and the second inequality is removed.

A useful generalization handles nearly parallel rows that differ only on singleton variables: one can substitute the singleton out (turning the rows into truly parallel rows of strictly smaller support) and then apply the above rules. The substitution requires tightening the singleton's bounds first, which is itself a useful side effect.

Detection is by hashing the row's support and normalized coefficients into buckets and comparing only within each bucket.

5.3 Non-zero cancellation

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §5.3

Adding a multiple of an equation to another row produces an equivalent model. If the multiple is chosen to cancel coefficients that the two rows share, the result is a sparser row.

Concretely, suppose AqS​xS​+AqT​xT​+AqU​xU​ArS​xS​+ArT​xT​+ArV​xV​​=bq​∘r​br​​ where S,T,U,V partition the joint support and AqS​ is parallel to ArS​ via s, i.e. sAqS​=ArS​. Subtracting s times the equation from the inequality eliminates the S-block from the second row, leaving (ArT​−sAqT​)xT​−sAqU​xU​+ArV​xV​∘r​br​−sbq​. The new row has ∣U∣ extra non-zeros from the U-block but ∣S∣ fewer from S, so the operation is profitable when ∣S∣>∣U∣.

The motivation is computational: many MIP solver subroutines scale with the number of non-zeros in the matrix, so a sparser model often runs faster even at constant feasible region. A side benefit is that the cancellation may produce singleton columns or rows, which then unlock further presolve.

The scalar s used in the cancellation must be moderate (∣s∣ not too large), or finite-precision arithmetic on the new coefficients introduces numerical issues that hurt later LP solves.

5.4 Multi-row bound and coefficient strengthening

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §5.4

Single-row bound and coefficient strengthening (§3.2 / §3.3) work from row activities computed by treating each variable's range as [ℓj​,uj​] independently. The activity bound this produces is, in general, slack — many points in the box ∏j​[ℓj​,uj​] are infeasible because of the other constraints.

Multi-row strengthening computes activity bounds using a small subset of constraints rather than just the bounds. Concretely, partitioning the support of row i into blocks S1​,…,Sd​ and computing ℓiSp​​≤AiSp​​xSp​​≤uiSp​​ for each block — possibly via an LP solve over the block's constraints — yields a tighter aggregate bound ℓiS​=∑p​ℓiSp​​ than the single-row approach. The new aggregate bound feeds into the §3.2 / §3.3 formulas verbatim.

The extreme version is optimization-based bound tightening (OBBT): solve min{xk​:x∈P} and max{xk​:x∈P} over a relaxation P (e.g., the LP relaxation, or a partial LP using a subset of rows) for the variable of interest. OBBT delivers the best possible LP-implied bounds at the cost of two LP solves per variable.

5.5 Clique merging

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §5.5

A clique in the conflict graph is a set of (possibly complemented) binary variables no two of which can simultaneously equal 1. Every set-packing constraint ∑j∈S​xj​+∑j∈T​(1−xj​)≤1 corresponds directly to a clique on S∪T, and the conflict graph aggregates this information across all such constraints in the model.

Clique merging combines several set-packing constraints whose underlying cliques can be unified into a single, larger clique. The procedure has two steps:

  1. Extension. Starting from a set-packing constraint, search the conflict graph for additional binary variables that, together with the constraint's variables, still form a clique. The constraint is extended by those variables.
  2. Subsumption. Other set-packing constraints whose cliques are now contained in the extended clique are dominated and can be discarded.

Paper Example 7 illustrates the simplest case: the three constraints x1​+x2​≤1, x1​+x3​≤1, x2​+x3​≤1 each describe a 2-clique on {x1​,x2​,x3​}. The conflict graph contains the triangle x1​,x2​,x3​, so any one of the three constraints can be extended to x1​+x2​+x3​≤1, after which the other two are subsumed.

Merging produces fewer, longer rows that are stronger in the LP relaxation.

6.1 Multi-column dual fixing for single equations

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §6.1

Single-column dual fixing (§4.4) only applies to variables that do not appear in any equation. Multi-column dual fixing generalizes the argument to recover a similar conclusion when the variable participates in exactly one equation r, as long as enough other variables in that equation can absorb its movement.

Concretely: let S+​⊆N be the variables s in row r with cs​≥0, ais​≥0 on every ≤-row, and ais​=0 on every other equation. These are the variables that "move freely upward" in the single equation r. Splitting S−​ analogously and letting S−C​ be the continuous variables among them, the condition inf{ArT−​​xT−​​}+sup{ArS−C​​xS−C​​}≥br​ (where T−​=N∖S−C​) certifies that the variables in S+​ can be fixed to their lower bounds without violating row r — any decrease in row r's activity from doing so can be compensated by increasing the continuous variables in S−C​.

The structural conditions are demanding, so the reduction triggers on a small fraction of models. When it does fire, it can fix many variables at once.

6.2 Fix redundant penalty variables

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §6.2

A penalty variable xp​ in a ≤-constraint is a singleton variable with positive cost cp​>0 and a negative coefficient arp​<0 in the constraint — its purpose is to absorb infeasibility in the constraint at a cost. Real-world models often introduce several such penalty variables for the same constraint, with different cost-per-unit-violation tradeoffs.

Sort the penalties by their normalized cost cp​/∣arp​∣: ∣ar1​∣c1​​≤∣ar2​∣c2​​≤…≤∣arp​∣cp​​. The cheap penalties are used first; the expensive ones never. If for some prefix k the cheaper k penalties at their upper bounds already make the constraint feasible, i.e. sup{ArV​xV​}+t=1∑k​art​ut​+t=k+1∑p​art​ℓt​≤br​, then every penalty xk+1​,…,xp​ is fixed to its lower bound ℓt​ — these expensive penalties are never needed.

The reduction generalizes to integer penalties with identical weights; non-identical integer weights would require divisibility arguments not typically implemented.

6.3 Parallel columns

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §6.3

Two columns A⋅j​ and A⋅k​ (with j=k) are parallel if there is a scalar λ=0 with A⋅k​=λA⋅j​. If, additionally, the objective coefficients satisfy ck​=λcj​ and the integrality / bound types are compatible, the two variables can be merged into a single new variable y:=xj​+λxk​. Every constraint sees only the combination aij​(xj​+λxk​)=aij​y, and the objective sees only cj​(xj​+λxk​)=cj​y, so the model can be rewritten in y alone.

The bounds on y depend on λ and the bounds of xj​,xk​: ℓy​={ℓj​+λℓk​ℓj​+λuk​​λ>0λ<0​,uy​={uj​+λuk​uj​+λℓk​​λ>0λ<0.​

Integer variables require extra care: even if the merged y is integer, splitting y⋆ back into integer xj⋆​,xk⋆​ at uncrush time may not be possible unless the image of xj​+λxk​ over the variables' domains is an interval of integers without holes. A sufficient condition is ∣λ(uk​−ℓk​)∣≥1 when one of the variables is integer and the other continuous.

Paper Example 8 — the LP min2x1​+4x2​+x3​ with column 2 parallel to column 1 via λ=2 — is a clean instance.

6.4 Dominated columns

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §6.4

In a model with all ≤ constraints, xj​ dominates xk​ (written xj​≻xk​) if

  1. cj​≤ck​ (using xj​ is no more expensive than using xk​),
  2. aij​≤aik​ in every row (using xj​ never makes any ≤ constraint harder than using xk​ would),
  3. j∈N∖I or k∈I (the integrality types are compatible — a continuous variable cannot be dominated by an integer one without losing options).

Domination supports several fixings (with infinite-bound preconditions):

  • If uj​=∞ and xj​≻xk​, then xk​ can be set to ℓk​. (Anywhere xk​ was used, swap to xj​ instead — feasible and no worse for the objective.)
  • If uj​=∞ and xj​≻−xk​ (the complement), then xk​ can be set to uk​.
  • Symmetric cases with ℓk​=−∞ go the other direction.

The infinite-bound requirement looks restrictive but the implied-free check (§4.5) often supplies effective infinity: if no other constraint needs uj​ to bound xj​, the explicit bound can be removed without losing feasibility.

7.1 Aggregate pairs of symmetric variables

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §7.1

A permutation π:N→N of the variable indices is a symmetry generator of the model if relabeling the columns by π (and possibly the rows by some companion row permutation σ) leaves the model unchanged: same objective coefficients on permuted indices, same bounds, same integrality, same A entries up to (σ,π).

If π has the additional property that no two distinct variables in the same orbit appear in any row together — formally, supp(A⋅j​)∩supp(A⋅π(j)​)=∅ for every j moved by π — then π is non-overlapping. For non-overlapping symmetry generators the orbit's variables are interchangeable in every constraint and can be aggregated: replace every variable in an orbit with a single representative xj​.

For continuous-only symmetry generators (those that move only continuous variables), aggregation is also valid; the aggregated variable represents the arithmetic mean of the orbit. This averaging step does not preserve integrality, which is why integer orbits require the non-overlap property.

Paper Example 9 — a small binary IP with two pairs of symmetric variables — illustrates the aggregation cleanly.

Symmetry detection itself is a graph-isomorphism problem on the row/column incidence graph and is solved using off-the-shelf tools.

7.2 Probing

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §7.2

Probing tentatively sets a binary variable xk​ to 0 and to 1, runs domain propagation under each assumption, and harvests the consequences. Let ℓj0​,uj0​ be the bounds on xj​ deduced from xk​=0 (via §3.2 propagation), and ℓj1​,uj1​ those from xk​=1. Four kinds of conclusion follow:

  1. Infeasibility-driven fixing. If propagating xk​=0 produces infeasibility, then xk​=1 in every feasible solution, so xk​ is fixed. Symmetric for the other case.
  2. Implied substitution. If ℓj0​=uj0​ and ℓj1​=uj1​ for some j, then xj​=ℓj0​+(ℓj1​−ℓj0​)xk​ — xj​ is an affine function of xk​ and can be substituted out.
  3. Tighter global bounds. If both probes return finite bounds for xj​, then ℓj​:=min{ℓj0​,ℓj1​} and uj​:=max{uj0​,uj1​} are valid global bounds.
  4. Implications. Anything weaker than the above is recorded as the implication (xk​=0⇒ℓj0​≤xj​≤uj0​), used downstream by branching, separation of implied bound cuts, and propagation at nodes.

A useful refinement is lifting: if probing finds an implication xk​=1⇒s≥d>0 on the slack variable of an inequality Ai⋅​x≤bi​, then the inequality can be replaced by Ai⋅​x+dxk​≤bi​, which dominates the original and is tighter in the LP.

Probing is sequence-dependent (the propagation under one probe uses the bounds tightened by previous probes), so the order in which candidate variables are probed matters. Heuristics order candidates by the number of binary fixings they directly imply.

7.3 Disconnected components

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §7.3

Build a graph on the variables where two variables are connected if they appear together in some constraint. The connected components of this graph correspond to independent sub-MIPs: if N partitions into U∪V with no constraint touching variables from both, then min{cUT​xU​+cVT​xV​:ARU​xU​≤bR​, AKV​xV​≤bK​} decomposes into min{cUT​xU​:ARU​xU​≤bR​}+min{cVT​xV​:AKV​xV​≤bK​}.

Two practical wrinkles complicate the simple "solve each separately" approach:

  1. No incumbent until the last component finishes. Solving components serially means the first complete feasible solution to the full model is only available after every component but the last has been solved. For long-running components this is undesirable.
  2. Gap limits don't decompose. A 0.01% optimality-gap target on the full objective doesn't translate cleanly to per-component gap targets, so any nonzero gap setting requires careful book-keeping.

A common compromise is to solve all but the largest component to true optimality during presolve (with strict work limits as protection against pathologically hard small components), fix their variables in the master, and then solve the residual in the normal MIP loop. If a component is too hard, the procedure aborts and falls back to an interleaved per-component search.

Disconnected components are sometimes intrinsic to the original formulation but more often arise after presolve has aggregated, fixed, or eliminated variables in the larger model.

7.4 Biconnected components

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §7.4

A biconnected component is a generalization of disconnected components (§7.3): instead of two pieces that share no variable, the model decomposes into two pieces that share exactly one variable — an articulation variable xk​. Fixing xk​ disconnects the rest.

Concretely, the model mincUT​xU​+cVT​xV​+ck​xk​s.t.ARU​xU​+ARj​xk​≤bR​, ASV​xV​+ASj​xk​≤bS​ splits, after fixing xk​=xˉk​, into the two independent sub-MIPs min{cUT​xU​+ck​xˉk​:ARU​xU​≤bR​−ARj​xˉk​},min{cVT​xV​:ASV​xV​≤bS​−ASj​xˉk​}.

If xk​ is binary, this is exactly probing (§7.2): solve both sub-MIPs at xk​=0 and xk​=1, and harvest

  • a fixing if one branch is infeasible, or
  • a substitution if both branches yield the same value of xj​∈V, or
  • a globally valid implication.

The sub-MIPs are smaller than the original, so the cost of probing the articulation variable is dominated by the savings of decomposing.

7.5 Exploiting complementary slackness

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §7.5

For the LP min{cTx:Ax≥b, x≥0} and its dual max{bTy:ATy≤c, y≥0}, optimality requires complementary slackness: xj⋆​>0⟹cj​−(A⋅j​)Ty⋆=0,yi⋆​>0⟹Ai⋅​x⋆−bi​=0.

For a MIP min{cTx:Ax≥b,ℓ≤x≤u,xj​∈Z for j∈I} with ℓ=0, although there is no general MIP duality, complementary slackness on the continuous part of the model can still produce useful reductions:

  • Apply bound strengthening (§3.2) to the dual constraints (A⋅S​)Ty≤cS​ over S=N∖I to derive bounds ℓˉ≤y≤uˉ.
  • For any row i with ℓˉi​>0, the dual variable is strictly positive in every optimal solution, so by complementary slackness the primal row is tight: Ai⋅​x=bi​. The inequality can be replaced by an equation.
  • For any column k with ck​>sup{(A⋅k​)Ty:ℓˉ≤y≤uˉ}, the dual feasibility constraint is strictly slack at every dual-feasible y, so by complementary slackness xk⋆​=0. The variable is fixed at its lower bound.

These reductions transfer information from the LP relaxation's dual into the primal MIP — variable fixings and tight equations that hold for every optimal solution.

Paper Example 10 walks through a 4-variable case where dual bound propagation derives both an equation and a variable fixing.

7.6 Implied integer detection

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §7.6

A continuous variable xj​ is implied integer if every feasible solution to the model satisfies xj​∈Z, even though the model does not declare xj​ as integer. Detecting this is useful: implied-integer variables can be branched on, used to derive stronger cuts (mixed-integer rounding cuts behave differently for integer vs. continuous variables), and exploited by domain propagation.

Two detection methods cover most cases:

  • Primal detection. If xj​ appears in an equation AiS​xS​+aij​xj​=bi​ with S entirely composed of integer or already-implied-integer variables, and bi​/aij​∈Z and ais​/aij​∈Z for all s∈S, then aij​xj​=bi​−AiS​xS​∈Z, so xj​ takes only integer values.

  • Dual detection. If the LP has an optimal vertex solution and the integer variables are fixed, the continuous variables form a vertex of the resulting "fixed LP". For a continuous xj​ that appears only in inequalities, the vertex condition forces at least one of those inequalities to be tight, or xj​ to be at one of its bounds. If the integer-fixed inequalities are themselves integer (after fixing) and ℓj​,uj​∈Z, then xj​ must take an integer value.

Once xj​ is marked implied integer, downstream cut generation, branching, and propagation treat it as integer — though the model formulation is unchanged.

8.1 Bound strengthening at nodes

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §8.1

Bound strengthening at the root (§3.2 / §5.4) tightens variable bounds globally; node bound strengthening does the same locally, using only the current node's branching restrictions.

The trigger is a branching decision: when the search creates a child node by adding xb​≤uˉb​ or xb​≥ℓˉb​, every constraint that contains xb​ has its activity bounds altered. The §3.2 propagation rules then derive new local bounds on the variables sharing those constraints, which in turn alter activities on other constraints, and the process iterates.

The reductions cascade: a single branching decision can trigger many local fixings and substitutions in the subtree. The effect is cumulative across the search — every node refines the bounds of its parent — and the cost is amortized by only revisiting constraints whose activity bounds actually changed since the parent.

Because node presolve runs at every search node, its per-call cost dominates correctness: the propagation passes are capped (typically a small fixed number of rounds), and only constraints that contain a recently-changed variable are revisited.

8.2 Orbital fixing

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §8.2

If a model has a symmetry group G — variable permutations that leave the model invariant (§7.1) — then for any feasible solution there are equivalent feasible solutions obtained by applying group elements. Branching that fixes one variable in a G-orbit creates a node at which the symmetry is partially broken, but the residual stabilizer subgroup still acts on the remaining variables.

Orbital fixing uses the residual symmetry to tighten the local node. For an orbit O under the residual group, any feasible point can be permuted so that the largest variable in O lies at any chosen index. In particular, the upper bound of every variable in O can be tightened to xj​≤min{uˉk​:k∈O}for all j∈O. A symmetric argument tightens lower bounds, though the bookkeeping is more involved.

Computing the residual symmetry group at every node is expensive. Cheap alternatives:

  • Filter the global generators. Discard generators of G that no longer respect the local lower bounds; the remaining generators generate a subgroup of the residual stabilizer, which is enough to derive valid orbital fixings.
  • Recompute from scratch at the node. Slower but exploits symmetry that the global group missed (e.g., symmetry introduced by branching).

Orbital fixing applies only to a subset of models — those with measurable symmetry — but on those models the per-node savings are large.

8.3 Conflict propagation

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §8.3

When a node's LP relaxation is infeasible, the dual ray of the failed simplex provides a Farkas certificate — a non-negative combination y≥0 such that yTA≤yTb contradicts the local bounds. Multiplying through and rewriting yields a conflict constraint that holds for the original (root) model — not just the failed sub-problem — and that excludes the configuration of bounds that caused the infeasibility.

Two flavors of conflict constraint are useful:

  • Bound-disjunction constraints. If the bounds in the conflict are all on binary variables, the conflict has the form ⋁k​(xk​=vˉk​). These are propagated efficiently using the two-watched-literals technique borrowed from SAT solving — only two variables in each clause are actively watched, and the clause is only revisited when one of them is fixed.
  • Linear conflict constraints. When non-binary variables are involved, the conflict is a linear inequality yTAx≤yTb. These are kept as regular constraints in the model and propagated like any other row.

Conflict constraints are useful primarily in the local neighborhood of the node where they were derived; their global utility decays quickly as branching moves the search elsewhere. Implementations therefore cap the number of conflict constraints retained, evicting the oldest or least-useful ones when the cap is hit.

8.4 Global probing

DRAFT — unreviewed. This chapter is a scaffolded starting draft. The math, instance, and prose have not been reviewed. See the §3.5 and §4.5 chapters elsewhere in the book for the target format.

Paper §8.4

Probing (§7.2) at the root only probes binary variables that are fractional in the root LP — variables that came out integer at the root are not probed, since there is no immediate need to refine them. As the search progresses and reduced-cost fixing or branching changes which variables are fractional, additional binaries become candidates for probing. Global probing at local nodes runs probing on these fresh candidates, using globally valid bounds (not the local node's bounds) to deduce globally valid implications.

The reductions are deliberately conservative. Unlike root probing, the local-node version

  • only collects implied bound information (no variable substitution, since substitutions would invalidate stored warm-start LP bases at sibling nodes),
  • only probes a binary the first time it becomes fractional during the search (each binary is probed at most once globally),
  • uses the current global bounds rather than local ones — the conclusions are valid throughout the entire tree, not just below the current node.

The output of global probing is exactly the same as root probing: globally valid bound changes, fixings, implications added to the implication graph, and statistics for branching variable selection. The difference is timing — these reductions are deferred to the moment they become available, rather than lost because the root LP did not flag the relevant variable.
